title: Word Ladder
description: A transformation sequence from word beginWord to word endWord is a sequence beginWord -> s1 -> s2 -> ... -> sk such that every adjacent pair of words differs by a single letter, and every si is in wordList. Note that beginWord does not need to be in wordList. Given two words, beginWord and endWord, and a dictionary wordList, return the number of words in the shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord, or 0 if no such sequence exists.
hints: Use BFS starting from beginWord, exploring all words that differ by one character.
Build an adjacency list or check neighbors on-the-fly by changing each character to a-z.
Track visited words to avoid revisiting.
timeComplexity: O(M^2 * N) where M is word length and N is number of words

title: Perfect Squares
description: Given an integer n, return the least number of perfect square numbers that sum to n. A perfect square is an integer that is the square of an integer.
hints: Think of this as a graph problem where each number is a node.
BFS from n, subtracting perfect squares to reach 0.
Level-by-level BFS gives the minimum number of squares.

title: Open the Lock
description: You have a lock in front of you with 4 circular wheels. Each wheel has the digits 0-9. The lock initially starts at '0000'. Given a list of deadends, if the lock displays any of these combinations, the wheels will stop turning and you will not be able to open it. Given a list of deadends representing deadend combinations and a target combination, return the minimum number of turns needed to reach the target. Return -1 if impossible.
hints: Model each 4-digit combination as a node in a graph.
BFS from '0000', generating neighbors by turning each wheel up or down.
Avoid deadends and previously visited combinations.
timeComplexity: O(10^4) since there are 10000 possible states

title: Is Graph Bipartite
description: Given an undirected graph with n vertices labeled from 0 to n-1, determine if the graph is bipartite. The graph is given as an adjacency list graph where graph[i] is a list of vertices adjacent to vertex i. A graph is bipartite if its vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets U and V such that every edge connects a vertex in U to one in V.
hints: Use BFS to color the graph with two colors.
If a neighbor has the same color as the current node, the graph is not bipartite.
The graph may be disconnected, so iterate over all nodes.

title: Rotting Oranges
description: You are given an m x n grid where each cell can be one of three values: 0 (empty), 1 (fresh orange), or 2 (rotten orange). Every minute, any fresh orange that is 4-directionally adjacent to a rotten orange becomes rotten. Return the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh orange. If this is impossible, return -1.
hints: Start BFS from all rotten oranges simultaneously (multi-source BFS).
Track the number of fresh oranges; decrement as they rot.
If fresh oranges remain after BFS completes, return -1.

title: Walls and Gates
description: You are given an m x n 2D grid initialized with three possible values: -1 (wall), 0 (gate), and INF (empty room, represented as 2147483647). Fill each empty room with the distance to its nearest gate. If it is impossible to reach a gate, leave the value as INF.
hints: Start BFS from all gates simultaneously instead of BFS from each empty room.
Multi-source BFS naturally finds the shortest distance to the nearest gate.
Update each empty room's distance as you visit it.

title: Graph Valid Tree
description: You have a list of n nodes labeled from 0 to n-1 and a list of edges where edges[i] = [ai, bi]. Return true if the edges form a valid tree. A valid tree is connected and has no cycles.
hints: A valid tree with n nodes must have exactly n-1 edges.
Use BFS to check connectivity from node 0.
If all n nodes are visited, the graph is connected and forms a tree.

title: Course Schedule
description: There are numCourses courses labeled from 0 to numCourses-1. Some courses have prerequisites, given as pairs [ai, bi] meaning you must take course bi before ai. Return true if you can finish all courses (i.e., no cycle in the dependency graph).
hints: This is a topological sort problem using BFS (Kahn's algorithm).
Compute in-degrees for all nodes and start BFS with nodes having in-degree 0.
If the count of processed courses equals numCourses, all can be finished.

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
description: Given an m x n matrix of heights, water can flow from a cell to an adjacent cell with a height less than or equal to the current cell's height. Pacific ocean touches the left and top edges, and Atlantic ocean touches the right and bottom edges. Find all cells where water can flow to both oceans.
hints: Instead of BFS from each cell, BFS backwards from the oceans.
Start from Pacific border cells and Atlantic border cells, flowing to higher or equal heights.
The intersection of cells reachable from both oceans is the answer.

solutions.python:
from collections import deque
def pacificAtlantic(heights):
    if not heights:
        return []
    rows, cols = len(heights), len(heights[0])
    pacific = set()
    atlantic = set()
    directions = [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)]
    def bfs(starts, reachable):
        queue = deque(starts)
        reachable.update(starts)
        while queue:
            r, c = queue.popleft()
            for dr, dc in directions:
                nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
                if 0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols and (nr, nc) not in reachable and heights[nr][nc] >= heights[r][c]:
                    reachable.add((nr, nc))
                    queue.append((nr, nc))
    pacific_starts = [(i, 0) for i in range(rows)] + [(0, j) for j in range(1, cols)]
    atlantic_starts = [(i, cols-1) for i in range(rows)] + [(rows-1, j) for j in range(cols-1)]
    bfs(pacific_starts, pacific)
    bfs(atlantic_starts, atlantic)
    return [[r, c] for r, c in pacific & atlantic]

title: Jump Game III
description: Given an array of non-negative integers arr, you are initially positioned at start index. From index i, you can jump to i + arr[i] or i - arr[i]. You are allowed to jump to an index with value 0. Return true if you can reach any index with value 0.
hints: Model each index as a node and jumps as directed edges.
BFS from the start index, exploring both forward and backward jumps.
Track visited indices to avoid cycles.
